planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in fall or early spring in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; southern live oak is highly salt-tolerant and drought-tolerant once established, making it ideal for coastal Gulf Coast landscapes.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; southern live oak is the quintessential evergreen tree of the Gulf Coast and Southeast, forming massive, wide-spreading crowns with horizontal limbs draped in Spanish moss that define the character of the region. This long-lived giant is remarkably tough — salt spray, poor sandy soils, periodic flooding, and coastal winds are all tolerated once the tree is established, and mature trees are exceptionally drought-tolerant. The small, dark acorns are produced in generous quantities and are critically important food for wood ducks, mallards, squirrels, and deer. Acorn production begins at 25 years and peaks in trees 75 years and older, underscoring that this is a multigenerational planting that rewards patience with an irreplaceable landscape anchor.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in fall or early spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in full sun; water oak transplants readily and establishes faster than many other oaks, making it one of the most practical large native oaks for landscape use in the South.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately wet, slightly acidic, well-drained to periodically saturated soil in full sun; water oak is one of the most common and fastest-growing native oaks of the coastal South, a familiar tree in moist lowlands, bottomland forests, and along streams from New Jersey to Texas. Its variable leaf shape — from three-lobed and duck-foot-shaped on the same tree — is a distinctive identifying trait. Growth rates of 2 feet per year are typical in moist, fertile soils, making it one of the most practical large native oaks for quickly establishing landscape shade and wildlife habitat. The small, round black acorns are produced in abundance starting at 20 years and are heavily consumed by wood ducks, mallards, white-tailed deer, and squirrels. A widely used and highly adaptable street tree throughout the Gulf South.

planting.note: Plant small container trees in fall or early spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun; willow oak transplants more easily than most oaks and is one of the best native trees for urban street and lawn use in the Southeast.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun; willow oak is one of the most graceful and distinctive of the native oaks, bearing narrow, willow-like leaves that give the tree an unusually fine-textured, airy appearance very different from other oaks. It grows naturally in moist bottomland forests and upland slopes throughout the Southeast and is one of the most widely planted native oaks as a street and lawn tree, valued for its tolerance of urban soils, relatively fast growth (1–2 feet per year), and adaptability. The small acorns produced abundantly from age 15 onward are a critical food source for wood ducks, mallards, wild turkeys, and many songbirds. The fine foliage turns attractive golden-yellow in fall before dropping cleanly, and the tree's upright-oval form matures into a large, handsome landscape specimen.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in fall in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full sun; plant at least one male within 50 feet of female trees for berry production — one male can pollinate up to five females.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; American holly is the classic native evergreen tree of eastern North American woodlands, producing the familiar glossy, spiny-edged deep green leaves and brilliant red berries that embody the winter holiday season. Female trees require a male pollinator within 50 feet to produce the berry crops that feed cedar waxwings, American robins, mockingbirds, and over 20 other bird species through the winter months. The tree grows slowly into a pyramidal to broadly columnar form and tolerates partial shade better than most large trees, making it useful as a woodland edge specimen or understory accent. Named cultivars such as 'Satyr Hill,' 'Merry Christmas,' and 'Jersey Knight' (male) have been selected for heavy berry production and improved form.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in fall or early spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun; sweetgum transplants readily when young and establishes quickly, growing 2–3 feet per year in good conditions.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun; sweetgum is one of the most visually spectacular native trees for fall color in the South, producing star-shaped leaves that turn a stunning mosaic of yellow, orange, red, and burgundy-purple — often all colors simultaneously on a single tree — rivaling the best maples in fall display. It is a major component of bottomland and upland forests throughout the eastern United States and grows rapidly into a large, pyramidal to oval tree. The distinctive spiny spherical seed balls (gumballs) that persist through winter provide important food for goldfinches, pine siskins, and purple finches but can be a nuisance underfoot on lawns; fruitless cultivars such as 'Rotundiloba' are available for situations where the seed balls are problematic. The sweetgum is one of the most ecologically important trees for wildlife in the Southeast.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in fall or early spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; black tupelo develops a deep taproot and transplants best when young — avoid moving established trees.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately wet, slightly acidic, well-drained to periodically saturated soil in full sun to partial shade; black tupelo (also called blackgum or sourgum) is widely regarded as one of the finest native trees for fall color in the eastern United States, turning brilliant scarlet — sometimes the first tree in the landscape to color in early autumn — weeks before most other species. It is remarkably adaptable, growing naturally in both swampy bottomlands and dry upland ridges, and tolerates periodic flooding as well as moderate drought once established. The small blue-black fruits ripening in September are among the most intensely sought wildlife foods of the season, consumed by at least 36 bird species including wood thrushes, robins, cedar waxwings, and wood ducks. Plant while young as the deep taproot makes transplanting difficult once trees exceed 3 inches in trunk diameter.
